hard.c: Code, Architecture, and Patterns must be balanced
hard.d: Complexity Always Propagates in large systems

hard.q: What is kernel bypass networking (e.g., DPDK)?
hard.a: Routing network traffic around firewalls
hard.b: A technique for hiding network traffic from the OS
hard.c: Allowing user-space applications to process network packets without going through the kernel
hard.d: Using the kernel to bypass slow network switches

hard.q: What is differential privacy?
hard.a: A mathematical framework that quantifies privacy loss when sharing aggregate data
hard.b: Encrypting each user's data with a different key
hard.c: Storing personal data in separate databases
hard.d: A GDPR compliance technique for deleting user data

hard.q: What is Hyrum's Law in software engineering?
hard.a: All bugs will eventually be found given enough users
hard.b: Software complexity doubles every 18 months
hard.c: Any interface will eventually be misused
hard.d: With enough users, every observable behavior of your system will be depended on by somebody

hard.q: What is memory-mapped I/O?
hard.a: Using RAM as a substitute for hard drive storage
hard.b: Creating a map of all I/O ports on the motherboard
hard.c: Mapping device registers to memory addresses so the CPU accesses hardware via normal memory operations
hard.d: Storing I/O buffers in virtual memory

hard.q: What is a 'phantom read' in database isolation levels?
hard.a: Reading data from a table that has been dropped
hard.b: Reading corrupted data due to disk errors
hard.c: Accessing a read replica that is behind the primary
hard.d: A transaction seeing new rows inserted by another transaction during re-reads

hard.q: What is the purpose of Intel SGX (Software Guard Extensions)?
hard.a: Accelerating graphics rendering in software
hard.b: Providing hardware-based memory encryption for isolated code execution (enclaves)
hard.c: Extending the x86 instruction set for server workloads
hard.d: Improving SSD performance through hardware encryption

hard.q: What is 'tail call optimization'?
hard.a: A compiler optimization that reuses the current stack frame for recursive calls in tail position
hard.b: Optimizing the last line of every function for speed
hard.c: Removing unused code at the end of files during compilation
hard.d: Prioritizing the last task in a job queue

hard.q: What is a 'confused deputy' problem in security?
hard.a: When a privileged program is tricked into misusing its authority on behalf of an attacker
hard.b: When an admin accidentally grants the wrong permissions
hard.c: When two security tools conflict with each other
hard.d: When a firewall rule is misconfigured

hard.q: What is the primary advantage of an LSM tree over a B-tree for writes?
hard.a: LSM trees use less memory
hard.b: LSM trees don't need disk storage
hard.c: LSM trees convert random writes to sequential writes, improving write throughput
hard.d: LSM trees support more data types

hard.q: What is a 'zero-knowledge proof'?
hard.a: Proving you have no knowledge of a security breach
hard.b: An authentication method that requires no password
hard.c: A cryptographic proof that a system has zero vulnerabilities
hard.d: Proving a statement is true without revealing any information beyond its validity

hard.q: What is NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access)?
hard.a: A memory architecture where access time depends on the memory location relative to a processor
hard.b: A networking protocol for non-uniform data packets
hard.c: A database sharding strategy
hard.d: A type of SSD that has variable read speeds

hard.q: What is linearizability in distributed systems?
hard.a: Storing data in a linear structure
hard.b: Processing all requests in a single queue
hard.c: Scaling a system linearly with added resources
hard.d: Ensuring operations appear to execute instantaneously at some point between invocation and response

hard.q: What is the 'Swiss cheese model' applied to security?
hard.a: Layering multiple imperfect defenses so holes in one layer are covered by another
hard.b: Using multiple security products from different vendors
hard.c: A penetration testing methodology that finds holes in defenses
hard.d: Encrypting data with multiple algorithms sequentially

hard.q: What is the 'epoch' concept in distributed consensus protocols like Raft?
hard.a: The time it takes for a heartbeat round-trip
hard.b: A monotonically increasing term number that helps detect stale leaders
hard.c: The maximum duration a leader can hold power
hard.d: The interval between log compaction events

hard.q: What is 'heap spraying' in exploit development?
hard.a: Filling heap memory with copies of shellcode to increase the chance of hitting it
hard.b: Clearing the heap to prevent forensic analysis
hard.c: Spraying network packets at a target's memory allocator
hard.d: A technique for corrupting heap metadata to trigger crashes
